items.q: Are the customer and the consumer always the same person?
items.correct: No — the customer buys the product and the consumer uses it; a parent buying toys is the customer while the child is the consumer.
items.misconception: Whoever uses a product must have bought it.
items.random_wrong: Consumers are people who work at the checkout.
items.why: This distinction shapes who advertising should target, a key early marketing idea.

items.q: Is a 'want' the same as a 'need'?
items.correct: No — needs are essentials like food, water and shelter, while wants are desires such as branded trainers; marketing often works on wants.
items.misconception: Everything people buy is a need.
items.random_wrong: Wants are products that are out of stock.
items.why: The needs and wants distinction underpins later work on demand and persuasion.

items.q: Does advertising aim at everybody at once?
items.correct: Usually not — businesses identify a target market, the group most likely to buy, and design products and adverts to suit them, which uses money more effectively.
items.misconception: Good adverts try to appeal to every single person.
items.random_wrong: Adverts are assigned their audiences by the TV channel.
items.why: Targeting is the first marketing concept KS3 students meet; scattergun thinking blocks it.

items.correct: The person who uses the product
items.why: Correct answer: The person who uses the product. “The seller” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: The group a business aims at
items.misconception: A shop on the high street
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The group a business aims at. “A shop on the high street” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If 800 people are in a market and 200 are teens, what % are teens?
items.why: The real figure is 25. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
